info.name: Quotation System API
info.description:
API endpoints for the Vistra Quotation System (Stage 1 scaffold).

Two route groups exist today:
- `/api/health` — infra health check (DB connectivity via Prisma). Always reachable, bypasses tenant resolution.
- `/api/auth/*` — better-auth endpoints (email/password only, no social providers, no email delivery configured).

Tenancy note: this app resolves the organization from the request's Host subdomain (e.g. `acme-glass.localhost:3000`), but `/api/auth/*` is intentionally excluded from that host-based resolution (see `proxy.ts`) — the session cookie itself is the per-org isolation boundary. Instead, the org is encoded directly in the sign-in email as `{username}@{orgSlug}.internal` (see `lib/auth-utils.ts` → `toAuthEmail`). That means every request in this collection works against a single host (`baseUrl`) regardless of which org you're acting as — just change the `orgSlug`/`username`/`password` variables.

Seeded test data (from `prisma/seed.ts`, password `Seed1234!` for all): orgs `vistra`, `acme-glass`, `nordic-walls`, `clearline`; usernames `admin`, `member`, `distributor`, `architect` per org.

Session auth uses an httpOnly cookie (`qs.session_token`) set by Sign In — Postman's cookie jar handles this automatically for requests run from the app (not from a raw `curl` export) as long as they hit the same `baseUrl`.

Endpoints NOT included: social sign-in/callback (no providers configured), password-reset-request/email-verification (no email delivery wired up — see `lib/auth.ts`), and account-linking endpoints (no social providers to link).

variable.description: Swap to http://localhost:3000 for local dev (docker DB running, npm run dev).
One of: vistra, acme-glass, nordic-walls, clearline (seeded).
One of: admin, member, distributor, architect (seeded).
Seeded password for all seeded users.
Used by the Change Password request.
Fill in a session token (from List Sessions) before calling Revoke Session.

request.description: Queries the DB via Prisma. 200 with database: "connected" proves the app -> Prisma -> Postgres chain works. Always reachable regardless of host (bypasses tenant resolution).
better-auth's own built-in liveness endpoint — confirms the auth handler is mounted and responding, independent of the app's own /api/health.
The synthetic email encodes the org: {username}@{orgSlug}.internal — NOT a real email address (see lib/auth-utils.ts). On success, sets the qs.session_token cookie used by every other request below.

Returns the current session + user (including the domain additionalFields: organizationId, username, roleId, active, externalCompanyId) based on the session cookie. Requires Sign In to have run first.
Lists all active sessions for the signed-in user. Copy a token from here into the sessionTokenToRevoke variable to test Revoke Session.
Revokes one specific session by token (see List Sessions).
Revokes every session for the current user except the one making this request.
Revokes every session for the current user, including the one making this request (signs you out everywhere).
Lists auth provider accounts linked to the current user. This app only uses the "credential" provider (no social sign-in configured), so expect exactly one entry.
Updates the signed-in user's profile fields (e.g. display name).
Changes the signed-in user's password. Update the `password` collection variable afterward so subsequent Sign In requests still work.

Changes the synthetic auth email. Caution: this is the login key (see toAuthEmail) — changing it changes what you sign in with. requireEmailVerification is false, so this applies immediately with no confirmation step.
Permanently deletes the signed-in user. Destructive — only run against seeded/test data, and re-run `npx prisma db seed` to restore it afterward.

This app sets disableSignUp: true (lib/auth.ts) — users are provisioned server-side only (via prisma/seed.ts or a future admin flow), not via public self-signup. Expect this to fail. Kept in the collection to make that boundary verifiable, not because it's meant to succeed.
